content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

February 25, 1981

Helen Coney
Administrative Assistant to
Senator John V. Briggs
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Coney:

This is in response to your request that I confirm in
writing the advice which I provided to you over the telephone
on February 4, 1981.

You asked whether a legislator is required to report his
taking a minor child (granddaughter, niece) to Disneyland on
a pass provided by Disneyland on Legislator's Day at
Disneyland.

The Political Reform Act requires a legislator to report
any gift of $25 or more on his Statement of Economic Interests.
The term gift is defined by the Act as "any payment to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received." Government Code Section 82028. "Payment", in
turn, is defined in Section 82044 to mean "a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other
rendering of money, property, services or anything else of
value, whether tangible or intangible." Since the complimentary
tickets described in your letter entitle the holder to attend
specified events without paying the admission price charged
to other members of the public, such complimentary tickets
constitute items of value. Moreover, since the value of such
tickets is in the entitlement to attend specified events free
of charge, it makes no difference whether you use the pass for
your niece or for yourself. The tickets are "payments" within
the meaning of Section 82044, and since they are complimentary,

Helen Coney
February 25, 1981
Page Two

we can assume that members of the legislature have not provided
equal or greater consideration in return for them. Accordingly,
the complimentary tickets constitute gifts which must be reported
by each member on his or her Statement of Economic Interests.

Please feel free to call me if you have any further questions.

Sincerely,


Alice Hughes
Documents Examiner

AH:ma

STATE CAPITOL

(916) 445-3256

DISTRICT OFFICE
1443 N. MUSCATEL BOULEVARD
FULLERTON, CALIFORNIA 92635
(714) 879-2345

RONALD L FOX
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

HELEN M CONEY
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

CHAIRMAN,
SENATE SELECT COMMITTEE
ON GOVERNMENTAL
INVESTIGATIONS
SENATE REPORTS

FILE J FINANCE
AND FISCAL
INSTITUTIONS
BUSINESS AND PROFESSIONS
TRANSPORTATION

SENATE SELECT COMMITTEE
ON SMALL BUSINESS
ENTERPRISES

California Legislature

STATE SENATOR
JOHN V. BRIGGS
THIRTY-FIFTH DISTRICT

SENATE REPUBLICAN WHIP


February 6, 1981


Ms. Alice Hughes
F.P.P.C.
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Alice:

I talked to you on the telephone the other day, regarding
whether or not a legislator is required to report his taking a
minor child (granddaughter, niece) to Disneyland on a pass
provided by Disneyland or legislator's Day at Disneyland (a
free day for legislators, staff and families).

You referred me to Section 82028 of the Government Code.
I later asked how this applied and asked that you provide something
in writing to verify. You said that I should write for this
information, so -- please furnish me with verification in writing.

Thank you. Also, would like to have this as soon as possible.

Sincerely,

Helen Coney
Administrative Assistant to
Senator John V. Briggs

HC:hek

sections.question: You asked whether a legislator is required to report his taking a minor child (granddaughter, niece) to Disneyland and on a pass provided by Disneyland on Legislator's Day at Disneyland.
sections.conclusion: The complimentary tickets constitute gifts which must be reported by each member on his or her Statement of Economic Interests.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a legislator report complimentary Disneyland passes received on Legislator's Day as gifts on their Statement of Economic Interests, regardless of whether the pass is used by the legislator or a minor child?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, complimentary Disneyland passes must be reported as gifts because they constitute items of value (entitlement to attend events free of charge) exceeding $25, and it makes no difference whether the legislator or a minor child uses the pass.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is partially illegible with OCR artifacts and image quality issues. Question and conclusion sections extracted from fragmented text. Confidence reduced due to text clarity issues, but core legal question and answer are identifiable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked whether a legislator is required to report his taking a minor child (granddaughter, niece) to Disneyland and on a pass provided by Disneyland on Legislator's Day at Disneyland.

CONCLUSION: The complimentary tickets constitute gifts which must be reported by each member on his or her Statement of Economic Interests.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that complimentary Disneyland passes received on Legislator's Day must be reported as gifts on the Statement of Economic Interests, as they constitute items of value under the Political Reform Act regardless of who uses them.
